articles.title: Arsenal Lift Premier League Trophy After 22-Year Wait
articles.summary: Arsenal beat Crystal Palace 2-1 on the final day of the season to claim the Premier League title, ending a 22-year wait to be crowned champions of England. It is one of the most anticipated moments in the club's modern history. The win sets Arsenal up as serious contenders for European glory next season.
articles.content:
It finally happened. Arsenal are Premier League champions for the first time since 2004. A 2-1 win away at Crystal Palace on the final day of the season was all they needed, and they delivered. Twenty-two years of near-misses, heartbreaks, and "nearly" moments are officially over.

For context, the last time Arsenal lifted the league title, Thierry Henry was at the peak of his powers and José Mourinho had not yet managed a single game in England. A generation of Arsenal fans grew up never seeing their club lift this trophy. That wait is now done.

The setting felt almost poetic. Crystal Palace's ground is called Selhurst Park, but the Daily Mail called this moment a coronation at the Palace, and that framing lands perfectly. Arsenal did not just win the title, they claimed it on enemy turf, in front of a full away end of supporters who had waited their whole football lives for this day.

Now the focus shifts to Europe. Arsenal head into next season with the hunger of a side that knows how to win, and the quality to go deeper in the Champions League than ever before. English football has a new king, and Arsenal are not done yet.
keyPoints: Arsenal beat Crystal Palace 2-1 on the final day to win the Premier League title
It ends a 22-year wait for Arsenal to be English league champions
The last time Arsenal won the title was in 2004
Arsenal are now targeting European glory next season
The win is one of the most significant moments in the club's modern era

articles.title: Pope Leo XIV Drops First AI Encyclical, Tells Big Tech to Back Off
articles.summary: Pope Leo XIV published his first encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas, on Monday, making history as the first pope to personally present an encyclical at the Vatican. The document calls for tough regulation of AI, warning that its unchecked power threatens democracy, human dignity, and world peace. Anthropic co-founder Christopher Olah shared the stage with the pope, creating a fascinating moment where the church simultaneously challenged and engaged Silicon Valley.
articles.content:
Pope Leo XIV dropped what may be the most talked-about document of 2026 today. His first encyclical, titled Magnifica Humanitas, which translates to Magnificent Humanity, landed Monday at the Vatican's Synod Hall. An encyclical is one of the most important teaching letters a pope can issue, traditionally sent to bishops but often addressed to the whole world. Leo used his to fire a clear shot at the AI industry.

The headline demand is bold. Magnifica Humanitas frames AI as the new industrial revolution and calls for AI companies to be subjected to stricter state and international regulations, while urging broad public participation in shaping how the technology develops. Leo did not mince words about power either. He wrote that "it is not enough to invoke ethics in the abstract; robust legal frameworks, independent oversight, informed users and a political system that does not abdicate its responsibility are required" and that "a more moral AI is not enough if that morality is determined by a few."

The timing of all this is loaded with symbolism. Leo signed the encyclical on May 15, the 135th anniversary of Pope Leo XIII's Rerum Novarum, the 1891 document that shaped modern Catholic social teaching around labor, capital, and the social consequences of the Industrial Revolution. Pope Leo XIV chose the name Leo precisely for this parallel. He told cardinals he had chosen the name Leo in light of Leo XIII's response to the first Industrial Revolution and the church's need to address "another industrial revolution" brought about by AI.

The guest list at the launch raised eyebrows across the tech world. The pope presented the document alongside high-ranking Vatican prelates, Catholic theologians, and Chris Olah, the co-founder of Anthropic, the American AI company behind Claude, which has been pushing to brand itself as safety-oriented and ethical. Some critics called Olah's presence a papal stamp of approval. But in February, the Trump administration ordered all U.S. agencies to stop using Anthropic's technology after it refused to allow the U.S. military unrestricted use of it, and Anthropic is currently suing the administration. So having Olah on stage alongside a pope who declared it "not permissible" to entrust lethal decisions to AI was a pointed move by everyone involved.

The Vatican has been building toward this moment for years. Technology professionals based in Silicon Valley alerted the Holy See to what was coming as far back as 2016. Under Pope Francis, Rome hosted AI ethics work involving Microsoft, IBM, and Cisco through the Rome Call for AI Ethics, and more recent Vatican-linked AI events have included representatives from Google, IBM, OpenAI, Cohere, and Anthropic. But Magnifica Humanitas is a step beyond networking. Pope Leo XIV is the first pontiff to personally present an encyclical letter to the world at the Vatican. Past popes have normally handed that role to cardinals or other senior figures. The message is clear: this one is too important to delegate.
keyPoints: Pope Leo XIV published Magnifica Humanitas, the first papal encyclical on AI, on May 25, 2026
The document calls for strict regulation of AI companies and warns against concentrating AI power in too few private hands
Leo declared it not permissible to entrust irreversible, lethal decisions to AI systems
Anthropic co-founder Christopher Olah shared the stage at the launch, despite the encyclical criticising Big Tech
Leo is the first pope in history to personally present an encyclical at the Vatican

articles.title: Pep Guardiola's Emotional City Farewell: 'Why Do You Love Me So Much?'
articles.summary: Pep Guardiola brought his 10-year reign at Manchester City to an end with an emotional farewell after a 2-1 home defeat to Aston Villa on the final day of the 2025-2026 season. The Etihad's North Stand was renamed the Pep Guardiola Stand in his honour, with a record 60,332 fans in attendance. Guardiola leaves having won 20 trophies, including six Premier League titles and the 2022-23 Champions League.
articles.content:
Ten years. Twenty trophies. One unforgettable goodbye. Pep Guardiola's time as Manchester City manager is over, and the farewell he was given on Sunday was the kind you only see once in a lifetime.

Manchester City and Pep Guardiola bid farewell to one of the most iconic partnerships in modern football, with the legendary manager bowing out with a 2-1 defeat against Aston Villa. Ollie Watkins scored twice as Villa came from behind to win on an emotional afternoon at the Etihad Stadium. The result barely mattered. Every person inside that ground knew the real story of the day was saying goodbye to the man who changed everything.

Across a decade, Guardiola oversaw 20 trophies, including six Premier League titles, three FA Cups and the 2022-23 Champions League. When he arrived in 2016, Manchester City were a rich club with big ambitions but no sustained dominance. By the time he walked out the door, they were one of the greatest club sides ever assembled. The Etihad's expanded North Stand was renamed the Pep Guardiola Stand, with a record 60,332 in attendance chanting for "10 more years."

Guardiola's words after the final whistle were pure emotion. "It has been an incredible, tremendous honour to be your manager for 10 years. Incredible emotions," Guardiola said after the match. He had been open all week about the reasons for leaving. "When you lose you are more sad and disappointed but in three days you have another. If you feel fatigue and everything's not as pleasant, you have to leave. It's not one person, decision, it's a question of time. Ten years is a lot of time."

Sunday also served as the farewell game for Bernardo Silva and John Stones, two of the most decorated players of the Guardiola era, adding an emotional weight to the occasion. And there was one more touching detail from the week. Sir Alex Ferguson sent Guardiola a voicemail congratulating him on the incredible journey he built during nearly a decade in charge of Manchester City, as well as the elite standards the club reached under his leadership. A legend from the red side of the city, acknowledging the man who built the blue dynasty.

Guardiola joked about his continued presence even after stepping down, saying: "The players don't know it yet but I will be there in 'The Pep Guardiola Stand' controlling them." That got a big laugh. But the tears were real too. This was not just a manager leaving a job. This was the end of an era that redefined what English football could look like.
keyPoints: Pep Guardiola ended his 10-year Manchester City tenure after the final day of the 2025-2026 season
City lost 2-1 to Aston Villa in Guardiola's last game, with Ollie Watkins scoring twice
Guardiola won 20 trophies at City including six Premier League titles and the 2022-23 Champions League
The Etihad's North Stand was officially renamed the Pep Guardiola Stand
Bernardo Silva and John Stones also played their final City games on the same day

articles.title: Trump's White House App Forced onto All Federal Employee Phones
articles.summary: The Trump administration is ordering federal agencies to install the official White House app on every government-issued phone, with the Federal Aviation Administration confirming automatic rollout starting this week. Cybersecurity experts are calling the move "dangerous," warning the app could open backdoor access to government networks, while former officials say it is simply a tool to push political messaging to federal workers. The same app is freely available to the public and includes a button that pre-fills a "Greatest President Ever!" text to send to Donald Trump.
articles.content:
The Trump administration has decided that every single federal employee's work phone needs to have the White House app on it. The White House recently unveiled the app to give the public "unfiltered" access to key priorities and policy updates, launching it in March. Now it is directing agencies to install it on the government phones of federal employees.

The Federal Aviation Administration told employees on Friday that its IT team "will automatically install 'The White House' application on all FAA-issued iPhones and iPads, as mandated by the White House," adding that the process would occur automatically and employees "do not need to take any action." The FAA is the first confirmed agency to roll this out, but the order covers the entire executive branch.

So what is actually in this app? The app includes official statements and policy announcements from the administration, as well as a feed of social media posts from White House accounts and the president. The app even gives you an option to "Text President Trump," which ultimately leads to signing up for a marketing blast. It also provides Donald Trump with a constant stream of praise if users click on the button to "text" the 79-year-old president, with a preloaded draft message featuring the phrase "Greatest President Ever!"

Experts are not impressed. Former government tech official David Nesting pointed out that the app being installed is the exact same one available to the general public, saying: "It's just making sure all federal employees are forced to see the same propaganda they push out to the public." The White House app could provide privacy risks, according to some cybersecurity experts. Following the app's release in March, early reports found that the app used location tracking and raised concerns about how it could share personal data with third-party sources. Now that the app will be found on government employee phones, it could introduce even more security vulnerabilities.

The order was communicated by Federal CIO Greg Barbaccia, requiring IT teams to assist in deploying the software across executive branch phones. The Hatch Act of 1939 requires all federal employees to maintain political neutrality, specifically "to protect federal employees from political coercion in the workplace." Critics argue the app's overtly political content puts the administration in direct conflict with that law. The White House has pushed back, with a spokesperson saying pre-installed apps are a normal part of government devices.
keyPoints: The Trump administration is ordering all federal agencies to install the official White House app on government-issued phones
The FAA confirmed automatic installation on all FAA iPhones and iPads with no action needed from employees
The app includes a "Text President Trump" button that pre-fills a "Greatest President Ever!" message
Cybersecurity experts warn the app could create backdoor access to government networks behind the firewall
Former officials say the app is political propaganda, and critics argue it conflicts with the Hatch Act's neutrality rules

articles.title: Kostyuk Wins at French Open Hours After Missile Hits 100m From Parents' Home
articles.summary: Ukrainian tennis player Marta Kostyuk won her French Open first-round match on Sunday just hours after learning that a Russian missile had destroyed a building just 100 metres from her parents' home in Kyiv. The No. 15 seed beat Oksana Selekhmeteva 6-2, 6-3 and broke down in tears at the end, dedicating the win to the people of Ukraine. Russia launched one of its largest attacks on Kyiv that morning, involving roughly 90 missiles and 600 drones, killing four people and injuring 83.
articles.content:
Sport has a way of putting everything in perspective. For Marta Kostyuk, the French Open first round on Sunday was about as far from a normal tennis match as it gets.

Hours before her opening-round match, Kostyuk received news that a missile strike had hit just 100 metres from her parents' home in Kyiv, Ukraine. At her afternoon press conference, Kostyuk held up her phone and showed reporters a picture of the scene she had received at 8 a.m. local time, three hours before her match. She said her mother, sister and great-aunt were in the house when the missile struck.

The overnight attack on May 24 involved roughly 90 missiles and 600 drones, standing as one of the largest and most destructive strikes against Kyiv and surrounding regions in the past year. Russia engaged in a large-scale attack on the Ukrainian capital that Sunday morning, and Ukrainian officials said four people were killed and 83 people were injured. Knowing her family was physically safe, Kostyuk said she never considered withdrawing from her match but acknowledged it was hard to concentrate on tennis.

Kostyuk, the No. 15 seed, was visibly emotional at the end of her match, a 6-2, 6-3 victory over Oksana Selekhmeteva, and appeared to be crying in her chair before her on-court interview. She told the crowd she was proud of herself, called it one of the most difficult matches of her career, and ended her speech with "Slava Ukraini" — Glory to Ukraine. In her on-court interview, Kostyuk said she didn't "want to talk about myself" and that "all of my thoughts and all my heart goes to the people of Ukraine today."

Kostyuk admitted that during the match she kept returning to the thought: "If it was 100 meters closer, I probably wouldn't have a mom and a sister today." Kostyuk, who has been outspoken about the Russian invasion of her home country since the conflict began in 2022, said this was the closest the fighting had been to her family home. Kostyuk enters Roland Garros on a 12-match win streak, having won back-to-back titles at the 250-level Open de Rouen and the 1000-level Madrid Open. Whatever happens next, Sunday's match will be remembered as one of the most emotionally charged moments of her career.
keyPoints: A Russian missile destroyed a building 100 metres from Kostyuk's parents' Kyiv home hours before her match
Her mother, sister and great-aunt were inside the house at the time but were unharmed
Kostyuk won her first-round match 6-2, 6-3 despite the devastating news
The attack on Kyiv that morning was one of the largest in the past year, killing 4 and injuring 83
Kostyuk dedicated her win to the people of Ukraine and ended her speech with 'Slava Ukraini'

articles.title: Star Citizen Tops $1 Billion as Squadron 42 Enters Closing Stages
articles.summary: Cloud Imperium Games has confirmed that Star Citizen has crossed $1 billion in lifetime funding, a historic first for any crowdfunded video game. In an exclusive interview with Variety, Cloud Imperium chiefs revealed the fan impact behind the milestone and confirmed that the long-awaited single-player campaign Squadron 42 is in its closing stages of development. The news comes alongside the controversial sale of a $5,000 virtual spaceship that is not yet flyable in-game.
articles.content:
Cloud Imperium Games just hit a number that nobody in the games industry has ever hit before. Star Citizen, the sprawling online space simulation that has been in development for over a decade, has now raised $1 billion in lifetime funding. The company marked the milestone on Sunday, May 24, and used the moment to give fans the most substantive update on Squadron 42 in years.

Squadron 42 is the single-player story campaign set within the Star Citizen universe. It has been anticipated since the early days of the project and features a star-studded cast. Cloud Imperium Games telling Variety that it is now in its "closing stages" is a significant statement, and fans who have been waiting years for this game will feel the weight of those two words.

Cloud Imperium Games marked the major milestone on Sunday, May 24, as Star Citizen reached $1 billion in lifetime funding, even before formalizing a release date. The fact that a game without a confirmed launch date has raised ten figures from its community says everything about the loyalty and passion of the Star Citizen fanbase. These are people who believe in the project deeply enough to spend real money on virtual ships, some costing thousands of dollars.

And about those ships: Star Citizen just hit $1 billion in funding, and the milestone came shortly after a new $5,000 virtual spaceship that players cannot fly in-game went on sale. The timing was eyebrow-raising to say the least. Cloud Imperium Games has always funded Star Citizen this way, selling ships and items that exist as future promises as much as current content. The model is divisive but clearly effective.

What Cloud Imperium chiefs told Variety about the fan impact is telling. The $1 billion did not come from a handful of whales alone. It came from a global community of backers who have been chipping in for years, buying ships, pledges, and game packages. The average backer is not spending $5,000. But enough people are spending enough money that Cloud Imperium Games has become one of the best-funded independent game studios on earth.

The question everyone now wants answered is simple: when? Squadron 42 being in its "closing stages" is promising, but Cloud Imperium Games has not set a release date for either Squadron 42 or the full persistent universe version of Star Citizen. With $1 billion raised and the world watching, the pressure to finally deliver has never been higher.
keyPoints: Star Citizen has crossed $1 billion in total lifetime crowdfunded funding as of May 24 2026
Cloud Imperium Games confirmed Squadron 42 the single-player campaign is in its closing stages
A $5,000 virtual spaceship that cannot yet be flown in-game went on sale around the same milestone
Star Citizen still has no formal release date despite more than a decade in development
The game is funded by a global community of backers purchasing virtual ships and game packages

articles.title: Felix Rosenqvist Wins Indy 500 by 0.0233 Seconds, Closest Finish Ever
articles.summary: Felix Rosenqvist won the Indianapolis 500 in the closest finish in the race's entire history, beating David Malukas by just 0.0233 seconds. The margin is so tight it is hard to even picture — less than the blink of an eye separating first from second. It is a moment that will be talked about in motorsport for decades.
articles.content:
The Indianapolis 500 has been running since 1911. In all that time, there has never been a finish like the one Felix Rosenqvist just delivered.

Rosenqvist crossed the line ahead of David Malukas by 0.0233 seconds, the tightest margin in the history of the race. To put that in perspective, a blink of a human eye takes around 150 to 400 milliseconds. The gap between first and second place on Sunday was 23.3 milliseconds. These two drivers were essentially side by side at the finish line, and only a photo finish camera could separate them.

The Indianapolis 500, for those less familiar, is one of the crown jewels of world motorsport. It is part of what is called the Triple Crown of Motorsport alongside the Monaco Grand Prix and the 24 Hours of Le Mans. Run on the iconic 2.5-mile Indianapolis Motor Speedway oval, it draws hundreds of thousands of fans every year and carries enormous prestige in American racing culture. To win it is a career-defining moment. To win it in the closest finish ever recorded makes Rosenqvist's victory something truly historic.

Rosenqvist, the Swedish driver, has been a consistent presence in IndyCar but this is the kind of win that rewrites a career narrative entirely. David Malukas, the American driver who came agonisingly close, will think about those 0.0233 seconds for a very long time. Sport is often cruel, and IndyCar does not get more dramatic than this.
keyPoints: Felix Rosenqvist won the Indianapolis 500 by just 0.0233 seconds over David Malukas
It is the closest finish in the 100-plus year history of the Indianapolis 500
The gap between first and second was less than 25 milliseconds
The Indianapolis 500 is one of the most prestigious races in world motorsport
Rosenqvist is a Swedish driver competing in IndyCar

articles.title: Drake Makes ARIA History With Three Albums in Top 10 at Once
articles.summary: Drake has become the first artist to debut three new albums inside the ARIA Albums Chart top 10 in the same week since the Australian charts launched in 1983. His triple drop of Iceman, Maid Of Honour, and Habibti landed at No. 1, No. 5, and No. 6 respectively. No artist in over four decades of ARIA history had pulled off this feat before.
articles.content:
Drake just rewrote Australian chart history. On the latest ARIA Albums Chart dated May 25, 2026, his three simultaneously released albums landed across the top 10 in one go. Iceman debuts at No. 1, Maid Of Honour bows at No. 5, and Habibti is new at No. 6, all via Republic/Universal. The ARIA Charts are Australia's official music rankings and have been running continuously since 1983, making this a record that stood untouched for over 40 years.

Drake surprise-released Iceman, Maid Of Honour, and Habibti on May 15 following the conclusion of his Iceman livestream series. He aired his fourth Iceman livestream on the eve of the album's release and ended it with the surprise announcement that he was dropping not one, but three new albums simultaneously, revealing the covers for each project before dropping the trio at midnight. The scale of that moment is hard to overstate: one artist, one night, three full albums.

The three albums generated more than 196 million Spotify streams worldwide during their opening day, while Iceman alone debuted with 140.2 million streams globally, marking the second-largest hip-hop debut in Spotify history behind only Certified Lover Boy. The impact also rippled across the ARIA Singles Chart. New tracks from Drake's three records scattered the chart, led by "National Treasures" at No. 4, "Janice STFU" at No. 5, "Whisper My Name" at No. 6, "Make Them Cry" at No. 9, and "Dust" at No. 10, with 15 Drake songs sitting in the top 50 this week.

The feat bests previous efforts by Guns N' Roses and Bruce Springsteen, according to ARIA. Guns N' Roses landed a double in 1991 when Use Your Illusion I and Use Your Illusion II debuted inside the top 10. Bruce Springsteen pulled off a similar two-album feat in 1992 with Human Touch and Lucky Town. Three albums at once, though? Nobody had ever done it. Drake now boasts 16 solo and collaborative top 10 albums on the ARIA chart, including Views, Scorpion, Dark Lane Demo Tapes, Certified Lover Boy, and For All The Dogs. Australia has clearly been along for the whole ride.
keyPoints: Drake is the first artist to debut three new albums inside the ARIA top 10 in the same week since the chart launched in 1983
Iceman hit No. 1, Maid Of Honour landed at No. 5, and Habibti debuted at No. 6
The three albums combined for over 196 million Spotify streams on their opening day alone
The previous record was a two-album debut, held jointly by Guns N' Roses in 1991 and Bruce Springsteen in 1992
Drake now has 15 songs inside the ARIA top 50 singles chart this week

articles.title: Antonelli Wins Canadian GP as Russell Retires With Title Lead Now 43 Points
articles.summary: Kimi Antonelli won a chaotic Canadian Grand Prix at Circuit Gilles-Villeneuve in Montreal on Sunday, claiming his fourth consecutive race victory of the 2026 F1 season. Mercedes teammate George Russell, who had been trading the lead with Antonelli for 30 laps, was forced to retire on Lap 30 with a power unit failure, leaving the 19-year-old Italian with a commanding 43-point championship lead. Lewis Hamilton finished second for Ferrari and Max Verstappen took his first podium of the season in third.
articles.content:
Kimi Antonelli is starting to look unstoppable. The 19-year-old Mercedes driver won the Canadian Grand Prix in Montreal on Sunday for his fourth straight victory of the 2026 Formula 1 season. Wins in China, Japan, Miami and now Canada have turned what looked like a tight title fight into something approaching a procession, at least for now.

The race itself was anything but straightforward. Antonelli and teammate George Russell put on one of the most entertaining driver-versus-driver battles in recent memory, swapping the lead multiple times across the opening 30 laps and coming dangerously close to making contact on more than one occasion. The tension had already been building all weekend. The two Mercedes drivers had made contact on Saturday during the sprint race, with Antonelli furious over the radio and demanding a penalty against Russell.

Then on Lap 30, it all went wrong for Russell in the cruelest possible way. His power unit suddenly failed and the 28-year-old Briton came to an abrupt halt at the side of the track. He threw his headrest across the circuit, slammed his fists into the front of the car, and ripped his gloves off in frustration. Russell said afterwards he wished he'd had "40 more of those laps" to battle Antonelli, adding: "I loved it. I thought it was great, and I've not had a battle like this in years." But he was also blunt about what the retirement means: "Right now the championship is his to lose."

With Russell out, a Virtual Safety Car was deployed and much of the field pitted. Antonelli held a solid lead over Max Verstappen, who went on to claim his first podium of the 2026 season in third. Second place went to Lewis Hamilton, who delivered his best result since joining Ferrari, finally overtaking Verstappen with six laps to go. It was a meaningful moment for Hamilton, who admitted: "It's been pretty tough for the past year and a bit, so to finally find our sweet spot and have a good weekend, it's an amazing feeling."

McLaren had a day to forget. Lando Norris, the reigning world champion, retired on Lap 40 with a gearbox issue, while Oscar Piastri finished 11th after a team strategy gamble on intermediate tyres badly backfired. Antonelli's championship lead now sits at 43 points over Russell with 17 races still to go. The next race is the Monaco Grand Prix on the weekend of June 5 to 7, kicking off the European leg of the season.
keyPoints: Kimi Antonelli took his fourth consecutive win of the 2026 F1 season in Canada
George Russell retired on Lap 30 with a power unit failure after leading the race and battling Antonelli
Antonelli now leads the Drivers' Championship by 43 points over Russell
Lewis Hamilton finished second for Ferrari, his best result since joining the team
Max Verstappen took his first podium of the 2026 season in third place

articles.title: Bryan Hubbard: The Ibogaine Lobbyist Who Texted His Way to the Oval Office
articles.summary: Bryan Hubbard, CEO of Americans for Ibogaine, is the little-known advocate whose lobbying convinced Joe Rogan to text Donald Trump about ibogaine, leading directly to Trump signing a landmark executive order on April 18, 2026. The order directed the FDA to fast-track review of psychedelic drugs including ibogaine, psilocybin, MDMA, and LSD, and earmarked $50 million for state governments to fund research. It is a remarkable story of one person working outside Washington who effectively set federal health policy through a podcast appearance and a presidential text message.
articles.content:
Bryan Hubbard is not a household name. He is the CEO of Americans for Ibogaine, a lobbying group pushing for wider access to a little-known psychoactive compound derived from a West African plant. But on April 18, 2026, Hubbard found himself standing in the Oval Office as Donald Trump signed an executive order that could reshape how America treats mental illness. The reason he was there? A text message.

Here is how it happened. Hubbard appeared on Joe Rogan's podcast and made his case for ibogaine. Rogan, one of the most listened-to voices in America, was convinced. He then texted Trump directly about the drug. Trump texted back almost immediately: "Sounds great. Do you want FDA approval? Let's do it." That exchange between Rogan and the president is what set the whole thing in motion, with Hubbard as the original spark.

The executive order Trump signed is a big deal. It directed the FDA to support clinical trials for psychedelics and speed up drug approval for substances including ibogaine, psilocybin, MDMA, and LSD, all of which are currently classified as Schedule I drugs with no accepted medical use. The order also allocated $50 million to state governments to advance psychedelic research. The FDA followed up by issuing Commissioner's National Priority Vouchers to three companies: Compass Pathways, Transcend Therapeutics, and the nonprofit Usona Institute, potentially cutting standard drug approval timelines from about a year down to just weeks.

Ibogaine has built a strong following among military veterans, who have sought treatment in countries like Mexico where it is legal. One veteran told CNN after his ibogaine session: "I won't say it healed everything overnight, but it gave me the ability to break free of the cycles that were holding me prisoner." The advocacy group Veterans Exploring Treatment Solutions says it has funded ibogaine treatment for over 1,300 veterans, with sessions that can cost upward of $5,000 each.

Not everyone is celebrating. Some psychedelic researchers have expressed concern about a top-down White House directive influencing what should be an FDA decision based purely on science. Previous ibogaine research had also stalled over concerns the drug could trigger fatal heart problems in some patients. And the $50 million price tag strikes some experts as far too small for the scale of research needed. Still, for Bryan Hubbard and advocates like him, a man who showed up to a podcast and ended up in the Oval Office, the moment represents something that felt impossible just a year ago.
keyPoints: Bryan Hubbard, CEO of Americans for Ibogaine, lobbied Joe Rogan, who then texted Trump directly about the drug
Trump signed an executive order on April 18, 2026, directing the FDA to fast-track review of ibogaine, psilocybin, MDMA, and LSD
The order allocated $50 million to state governments for psychedelic research
The FDA issued fast-track priority vouchers to Compass Pathways, Transcend Therapeutics, and the Usona Institute
Critics worry about White House pressure on FDA science and say $50 million is not enough for serious research
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Bryan Hubbard Americans for Ibogaine advocate

articles.title: Jordan Peele Asked Marlon Wayans to Roast Get Out in Scary Movie 6
articles.summary: Marlon Wayans revealed in a TikTok video that Jordan Peele personally reached out asking to have his films spoofed in Scary Movie 6, which hits theaters on June 5, 2026. Peele is not the only director to do so. Wayans says filmmakers regularly contact him requesting the parody treatment, which says a lot about how the Scary Movie franchise has shifted from target to badge of honor. The trailer already shows Wayans' character Shorty being pulled into Get Out's iconic "sunken place," so it looks like Peele is getting exactly what he asked for.
articles.content:
Here is something you probably did not expect. Jordan Peele, the filmmaker behind Get Out, Us, and Nope and one of the architects of modern prestige horror, reached out to Marlon Wayans and basically said: please make fun of my movies. Wayans confirmed the outreach in a TikTok video, and the detail quickly set film Twitter on fire.

Wayans explained that he hears from directors all the time with the same kind of ask. "I actually get directors going, 'Hey man, you think maybe you could put this parody in this movie or that movie,'" he said. Peele was one of them. And when you think about it, being parodied by Scary Movie is now a mark of cultural arrival. The franchise has spoofed Scream, The Exorcist, The Ring, and The Blair Witch Project. Getting the Wayans treatment means your film has broken through in a way that goes beyond box office numbers.

Wayans also explained the philosophy behind the humor in Scary Movie 6. In that same TikTok, he said the goal is never to tear down the films being parodied, but to have fun with them in a way that audiences and the original creators can both enjoy. That generous spirit is clearly why directors like Peele feel comfortable making the ask. Wayans put it plainly in a separate Entertainment Weekly interview: "What we're trying to do is bring back laughter. This is about bringing back comedy the way it used to be."

The trailer already shows Peele's influence all over Scary Movie 6. Wayans' character Shorty is seen being pulled into a couch in a direct spoof of the hypnosis scene from Get Out, where Daniel Kaluuya's character sinks into the "sunken place." The film is also taking aim at Ryan Coogler's Sinners, the horror hit Weapons, A Quiet Place, and even the Scream franchise. With Anna Faris and Regina Hall both returning as Cindy and Brenda, it marks the first time those two characters have shared the screen together since Scary Movie 2 in 2001.

Scary Movie 6 is directed by Michael Tiddes, with the script written by Marlon, Shawn, and Keenen Ivory Wayans. The Scary Movie franchise has earned $461 million at the global box office across its five previous films, and this sixth entry arrives more than a decade after Scary Movie 5, which came out in 2013. The long wait means there is a massive backlog of horror to parody. Expect Get Out's sunken place, Sinners, Longlegs, and a lot more to get the treatment when Scary Movie 6 opens on June 5, 2026.
keyPoints: Jordan Peele personally reached out to Marlon Wayans asking to have his films spoofed in Scary Movie 6
Wayans says multiple directors regularly contact him requesting the parody treatment
The trailer already includes a Get Out spoof with Wayans' character Shorty sinking into the sunken place
Scary Movie 6 also parodies Sinners, Weapons, A Quiet Place, and the Scream franchise
Anna Faris and Regina Hall return as Cindy and Brenda for the first time together since Scary Movie 2 in 2001

articles.title: PewDiePie Is Ending Family Vlogs So Son Björn Can Grow Up Offline
articles.summary: Felix 'PewDiePie' Kjellberg has announced he and his wife Marzia are ending their family vlog series on YouTube later this year, citing their son Björn's right to grow up without an internet audience. Björn turns three in July and is becoming more aware of the world around him, which prompted the decision. PewDiePie says Björn can choose to rejoin the content when he is old enough to make that call himself.
articles.content:
Felix Kjellberg, better known as PewDiePie, built one of the biggest audiences in YouTube history. At his peak he was the most-subscribed individual creator on the entire platform. Now the Swedish creator, who has been living in Japan with his wife Marzia since 2022, is drawing a firm line between his public life and his family's private one.

The decision comes down to one person: Björn. Kjellberg shared in his newsletter that his son Björn turns three in July and is slowly becoming more aware of the world around him. Kjellberg wants him to grow up away from the internet and not have to be recognised everywhere. That is a completely reasonable ask for any parent, and it carries extra weight when your dad has tens of millions of subscribers.

Kjellberg added that Björn can join in again when he is old enough to make his own choices, and he does not rule out the possibility that the family might still post something on social media from time to time. So this is not a full disappearing act. It is more of a deliberate reset of the boundary between what PewDiePie shares and what belongs only to the Kjellberg family. The change seems to apply only to the vlogs, so viewers will likely still see more PewDiePie videos, just with a different focus than the family.

PewDiePie's arc is worth pausing on. He registered his YouTube channel in 2010, primarily posting Let's Play videos of horror and action games, and the channel became the most-subscribed on YouTube on 15 August 2013. In May 2022, Kjellberg and Marzia moved from England to Japan, and much of his content following the move consisted of vlogs about daily life around Tokyo. That chapter gave fans a warmer, quieter version of PewDiePie. Now that chapter is closing.

Fans have responded with a mix of understanding and nostalgia, with many long-time viewers recognising that PewDiePie's content has naturally evolved over more than a decade, while others express disappointment at the reduced access to his personal life. There is also a broader awareness that creator boundaries are increasingly necessary in an era when personal content can quickly spread beyond its intended audience. PewDiePie is not alone in making this call. Plenty of big creators have quietly stopped putting their kids on camera as the conversation around children's digital footprints has grown louder. But coming from someone with PewDiePie's reach, the choice lands as a meaningful statement about where the creator world is heading.
keyPoints: PewDiePie and Marzia are ending their family vlog series on YouTube later in 2026
The reason is protecting son Björn's privacy as he turns three in July and grows more aware of the world
Björn will be able to choose to appear in content again when he is old enough to decide for himself
PewDiePie will continue making YouTube videos, just without the family vlog focus
The move reflects a growing trend of long-term creators pulling their children out of public content

articles.title: The Jacaranda Teams Up With Paul McCartney to Become The Maccaranda
articles.summary: Liverpool's legendary Jacaranda venue is changing its name to The Maccaranda after teaming up with Paul McCartney. The club holds a special place in Beatles history as the rehearsal space where the band found their footing in the late 1950s, and even where drummer Pete Best was auditioned. The name change is a full-circle moment for a venue that helped shape one of the greatest bands in the world.
articles.content:
Some venues are just buildings. The Jacaranda in Liverpool is something else entirely. It is the room where The Beatles rehearsed as teenagers in the late 1950s, and the place where Pete Best auditioned to become their drummer before Ringo Starr took the seat. Now, in a move that nobody saw coming, the venue is getting a new name: The Maccaranda, created in partnership with Paul McCartney himself.

The name is a clever blend of McCartney's surname and Jacaranda, and it cements a bond between the venue and the man that has existed for over six decades. McCartney's fingerprints were all over the early days of The Beatles, and they were all over this building in particular. Teaming up to rename it feels less like a marketing stunt and more like a homecoming.

For anyone who doesn't know the backstory, The Jacaranda was owned by Allan Williams in the late 1950s, who was one of The Beatles' earliest managers. The band used the basement as a rehearsal space when they were still figuring out who they were and what they wanted to sound like. It is genuinely sacred ground for music history fans.

The rebrand to The Maccaranda is the kind of story that Liverpool, a city that wears its Beatles heritage with enormous pride, absolutely loves. It ties a living legend directly to one of the city's most storied addresses, and makes sure that history stays alive for anyone walking through the door for the first time.
keyPoints: The Jacaranda in Liverpool is renaming itself The Maccaranda in partnership with Paul McCartney
The Beatles used the venue as a rehearsal studio in the late 1950s
Pete Best auditioned to be The Beatles drummer at the same venue
The name blends McCartney's surname with the original venue name
The move cements McCartney's lifelong connection to one of Liverpool's most historic music spaces
